does: This paper identifies and addresses task drift in retrieval-augmented LLM applications, where prompt injections embedded in external data cause models to deviate from user instructions. The authors propose a detection method based on activation deltas—differences in neural activations before and after processing external data sources. They develop two probing approaches using simple linear classifiers trained on these activation differences and demonstrate near-perfect ROC AUC performance in detecting task drift on out-of-distribution test sets, showing that activation patterns reliably capture when an LLM has been manipulated away from its original task.
connects: This work builds on growing research into prompt injection attacks and adversarial robustness of LLMs in real-world deployment scenarios. It extends prior work on detecting out-of-distribution inputs and anomalous behavior in neural networks by focusing specifically on the activation-space signatures of prompt injection. The paper connects to broader literature on mechanistic interpretability of LLMs and probing methods for understanding what information is encoded in different layers. It responds directly to practical vulnerabilities in retrieval-augmented generation systems, a class of applications that has become increasingly common as LLMs integrate with external data sources and knowledge bases.
enables: This activation-based detection approach opens pathways for real-time filtering of prompt injections in deployed retrieval-augmented systems, enabling safer integration of LLMs with untrusted external data. The work suggests that activation patterns could serve as a general-purpose anomaly signal for detecting when models deviate from intended behavior, potentially applicable beyond prompt injection to other forms of behavioral drift. Future research could explore lightweight deployment of these detectors in production systems, develop more sophisticated classifiers leveraging full activation geometry, and investigate whether activation deltas reveal the underlying mechanisms of how prompt injections propagate through model layers, contributing to mechanistic understanding of LLM vulnerability.
